summary: In a historic joint interview at the D5 conference, Steve Jobs and Bill Gates reflect on their contributions to the tech industry, the evolution of the PC, and the future of computing. Jobs emphasizes Apple's core identity as a software company, the power of tightly integrating hardware and software, and the importance of abandoning zero-sum rivalries to focus on inventing the future.

principles.statement: Apple is fundamentally a software company.
principles.rationale: Despite being known for beautiful hardware, the core value and differentiating factor of devices like the Mac, iPod, and iPhone is the software running inside them and in the cloud.
principles.source_quote: Apple views itself as a software company and there aren’t very many software companies left, and Microsoft is a software company.

principles.statement: Do not play zero-sum games with competitors.
principles.rationale: Believing that a competitor must lose for you to win is destructive, especially when the competitor is dominant. It distracts a company from remembering its own identity and strengths.
principles.source_quote: if the game was a zero-sum game where for Apple to win, Microsoft had to lose, then Apple was going to lose.

principles.statement: Focus on inventing tomorrow rather than worrying about yesterday.
principles.rationale: Dwelling on past mistakes, firings, or wrong turns is a waste of energy. Progress requires clearing out the cobwebs and looking forward.
principles.source_quote: let’s go invent tomorrow rather than worrying about what happened yesterday.

principles.statement: The marriage of rich client apps and cloud services is vastly superior to browser-only experiences.
principles.rationale: Dedicated client applications can leverage local processing power to deliver a user experience that blows away what is possible within a standard web browser.
principles.source_quote: the marriage of some really great client apps with some really great cloud services is incredibly powerful and right now, can be way more powerful than just having a browser on the client.

principles.statement: Hardware and software coupling works best in the consumer market.
principles.rationale: Outside of Windows PCs, decoupling hardware and software rarely results in a superior consumer experience. Tightly integrating them allows for better product design and functionality.
principles.source_quote: in the consumer market, at least, I think one can make a pretty strong case that outside of Windows on PCs, it’s hard to see other examples of the software and hardware being decoupled working super well yet.

mental_models.name: The Beautiful Box for Software
mental_models.description: Viewing hardware products not primarily as physical devices, but as vessels designed specifically to deliver a seamless software experience.
mental_models.example: Describing the iPod as just software on the device, on the PC, and in the cloud, wrapped in a 'beautiful box'.

mental_models.name: The Digital Hub
mental_models.description: Viewing the personal computer as the central repository and multimedia center that connects and manages a user's peripheral digital devices (cameras, music players, etc.).
mental_models.example: Using the PC to manage digital cameras, camcorders, and share things over the Internet, acting as the hub of a user's digital life.

heuristics: People that love software want to build their own hardware. (Quoting Alan Kay)
If you don't have to convince someone they have a problem your product solves, you have a great product.
Who cares what's inside it anymore? Focus on what it is and how the consumer approaches it.

signature_quotes.text: Apple views itself as a software company and there aren’t very many software companies left...
signature_quotes.context: Explaining Apple's core identity despite being known for hardware.

signature_quotes.text: if the game was a zero-sum game where for Apple to win, Microsoft had to lose, then Apple was going to lose.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why he decided to partner with Microsoft upon returning to Apple in 1997.

signature_quotes.text: let’s go invent tomorrow rather than worrying about what happened yesterday.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why he donated Apple's historical archives to Stanford upon his return.

signature_quotes.text: We’re getting to the point where everything’s a computer in a different form factor. So what, right? ... who cares what’s inside it anymore?
signature_quotes.context: Responding to whether smartphones are just computers in a different form factor.

signature_quotes.text: the marriage of some really great client apps with some really great cloud services is incredibly powerful and right now, can be way more powerful than just having a browser on the client.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against the idea that all computing will move entirely to the cloud/browser.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It causes a company to focus on destroying the competition rather than remembering its own identity and building great products, which is fatal if the competitor is much larger.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on web browsers for mobile applications.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Browsers cannot match the responsiveness, persistent state, and rich user experience of a dedicated client application running locally on the device.

anti_patterns.description: Looking backward and dwelling on past mistakes.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It wastes time and energy that should be spent inventing the future.
